arguments_against: Two strong arguments against. (1) The biography record is itself a conflation of two different men: version col1883-L29281.v ('ROBINSON, Sir WILLIAM C. F., K.C.M.G. (1877). (C.M.G., 1873)' — private secretary to Sir Hercules Robinson, president of Montserrat 1862, Falklands 1866, Prince Edward Island 1870-73, Western Australia 1874 and 1880, Straits Settlements 1877, South Australia 1882) and versions col1883-L29279.v/col1898-L35635.v ('ROBINSON, SIR WILLIAM, K.C.M.G. (1883), (C.M.G., 1877)' — a Colonial Office clerk 1854-1873, then Bahamas, Windward Islands, Trinidad 1885, Hong Kong 1891, b. 1836, ret. 1898). These two entries coexist in the SAME 1883 edition with mutually exclusive careers for 1854-1873 and conflicting honour dates, so they are two persons wrongly merged under one bio_id; any stint assignment to this bio_id risks attaching records to the wrong component. (2) Robinson is the commonest surname in the corpus (136 officials, 56 bios). Why the assignments still stand, against the W. C. F. component specifically: the Straits stint prints the full name 'Sir William C. F. Robinson' as 'Governor and Commander-in-Chief' with K.C.M.G. in 1878, matching 'governor of the Straits Settlements, Aug., 1877' and 'K.C.M.G. (1877)' exactly on office, colony, date, and honour — and the other William was then governing the Bahamas, so the partition is clean. The Canada stint ('William C. F. Robinson', 'Lieutenant-Governor', department 'Lieutenant-Governors since entering the Dominion', K.C.M.G.) is a retrospective Dominion list matching 'governor of Prince Edward Island, 5th July, 1870, to Nov. 1873' — PEI entered the Dominion in 1873, during his term; the other William never served in Canada. The WA stint ('Sir W. C. F. Robinson', Governor and C-in-C, G.C.M.G., 1890-94) continues the W. C. F. career shape (WA governor in 1874 and again Jan. 1880; historically he held a third WA term 1890-95) while the other William was then governor of Hong Kong per his own entry ('gov. of Hong Kong, 1891'), excluding him. Confidence is capped at probable solely because the bio entity is conflated and must be split before these links are published.

evidence_cited.fact: governor of the Straits Settlements, Aug., 1877
governor of Prince Edward Island, 5th July, 1870, to Nov. 1873

would_overturn: Confirmation that the bio_id is canonically the OTHER William Robinson (b. 1836, Bahamas/Trinidad/Hong Kong) rather than William Cleaver Francis Robinson — in that case all three assignments are wrong and must be moved to a new bio entity; or evidence of a third W. C. F. Robinson holding any of these posts.
notes: IMPORTANT FOR REVIEWER: bio 'robinson_william-c-f_b1836' conflates two distinct contemporaries. Versions col1883-L29279.v and col1883-L29281.v are two separate entries printed in the same 1883 edition: (A) Sir William Robinson, b. 1836, C.M.G. 1877/K.C.M.G. 1883/G.C.M.G. 1897, Colonial Office clerk then governor of Bahamas, Windward Is., Trinidad, Hong Kong, ret. 1898; (B) Sir William Cleaver Francis Robinson, C.M.G. 1873/K.C.M.G. 1877, governor of Falklands, PEI, WA (x3), Straits Settlements, South Australia. The printed birth year 1836 and the header honours belong to (A); all four candidate stints belong to (B). The bio entity must be split; the three assignments above attach to component (B). Confidence deliberately capped at probable to force human review of the split.

arguments_against: Strongest case against: (a) the bio record is internally conflated — version col1883-L29281.v (Sir William C. F. Robinson: Montserrat, Falklands, PEI, Western Australia 1874 and 1880, Straits, South Australia 1882) describes a DIFFERENT man from versions col1883-L29279.v/col1898-L35635.v (Sir William Robinson, b.1836, Colonial Office clerk, Bahamas, Windwards, Trinidad 1885, Hong Kong 1891); the b.1836 and G.C.M.G.(1897) attributes belong to the plain-William component, so the deterministic honour PASS (G.C.M.G.) is likely a cross-person artifact; (b) the printed entry never mentions a WA governorship in 1890-1894, so the link rests on inference, and a different Sir W. C. F. Robinson could in principle exist among 136 Robinsons in the corpus. Why it still holds: the stint name 'Sir W. C. F. Robinson' matches all three initials exactly; Governor and Commander-in-Chief of Western Australia is a unique office; and the C.F. component of the bio shows two prior WA governorships (1874 and Jan. 1880) plus South Australia 1882, making a return WA term 1890-1894 strong career-shape continuity. No other biography with initials W. C. F. is present. Probable rather than certain because the link is to the C.F. component of a conflated bio record and the 1890 term is not itself printed in the entry.

evidence_cited.fact: appointed governor of South Australia, Nov., 1882

would_overturn: Evidence of a second Sir W. C. F. Robinson holding the WA governorship 1890-1894, or evidence that the C.F. component of this bio (the Q-entity behind the case) was elsewhere during 1890-1894.
notes: IMPORTANT for reviewer: the biography record itself conflates two distinct historical persons. Versions col1883-L29279.v, col1897-L34112.v and col1898-L35635.v describe Sir William Robinson (b.1836; CO clerk; Bahamas; Windwards; Trinidad 1885; Hong Kong 1891; G.C.M.G. 1897), while version col1883-L29281.v describes Sir William C. F. Robinson (priv. sec. to Sir Hercules Robinson; Montserrat; Falklands; PEI; WA 1874 and 1880; Straits; South Australia 1882; K.C.M.G. 1877). The compiled timeline interleaves both careers. The WA 1890 stint is assigned to the C.F. component. The bio record should be split. Also, the South Australia stint chain conflates W. N. Robinson (Medical Officer) with the 1886 governor row and should also be split.

arguments_against: Strongest case against: Bell is the commonest surname imaginable in this corpus (123 officials, 69 biographies), and the stint's cluster key 'Francis Dillon Bell' — missing the 'Henry' — is exactly the name of this man's famous father, Sir Francis Dillon Bell, also a New Zealand K.C.M.G. So this stint looks, by name alone, like the father. This fails on hard chronology and office content: the stint runs 1920-1922, decades after the father's generation, and the offices listed are a verbatim mirror of the son's biography for precisely those years — the 1920 row bundles 'Attorney-General, Minister of Education, Minister of Public Health, Minister Immigration, Commissioner of State Forests ... and Leader of the Legislative Council', matching the bio's atty.-gen. 1918-26, 'min. of educn., min. of public health and min. of immigr., 1919-20', 'comnr. of state forests, 1919-22', and leader of legis. coun. 1912-26. The K.C.M.G. in every row matches the bio's 1915 award. No second Sir Francis Bell with this exact office bundle existed in 1920s New Zealand; the dropped 'Henry' is a clustering artifact of the staff list printing 'Sir Francis Bell'.

evidence_cited.fact: Attorney-General, Minister of Education, Minister of Public Health, Minister Immigration, Commissioner of State Forests
Attorney-General, Commissioner of State Forests, Minister in Charge of Valuation Department, and Leader of the Legislative Council

would_overturn: Evidence that a different Sir Francis Bell, K.C.M.G., held the New Zealand Attorney-Generalship and the State Forests/Valuation portfolios in 1920-22 — which would also contradict the bio's own dated office list.
notes: Both stints are fragments of the same person, Rt. Hon. Sir Francis Henry Dillon Bell (b. 1851, NZ prime minister May 1925), split by name-key variants ('Francis Bell' vs 'F. H. D. / Francis Henry Dillon Bell'); they should be merged downstream. The name-trap here is his father Sir Francis Dillon Bell, K.C.M.G., whom the first stint's cluster key superficially matches — excluded by chronology (stint years 1920-22) and by the office bundle matching the son's bio verbatim. The honours columns (K.C.M.G. from 1917, never G.C.M.G.) lag the bio's 1923 G.C.M.G. only because the stint ends in 1922.

arguments_against: Strongest case against: this stint spans 1897-1922 and mixes two seemingly different careers — a Foreign Consuls thread (Vice-Consul, then Consul for North Island, 1897-1922) and an Executive Council ministerial thread (1913-1922) — so one could argue the consul is a different Francis Henry Dillon Bell (a relative) and the cluster is contaminated. This fails: the consul rows carry the full distinctive triple name throughout, and from 1917 they acquire exactly the bio subject's honours and style — 'K.C.M.G.' from 1917 (bio: K.C.M.G. 1915), 'Sir Francis Henry Dillon Bell' by 1920, 'Hon. Sir Francis Henry Dillon Bell' in 1922, the 'Hon.' matching his Legislative Council leadership. A hypothetical second F. H. D. Bell who was also knighted K.C.M.G. in the same years is not realistic. The ministerial rows match the biography clause for clause: Minister of Internal Affairs 1913-15 (bio 'min. of internal affairs, 1912'), 'Minister of Immigration and Leader of the Legislative Council' 1917-18, Attorney-General 1919 (bio 1918-26), Minister of Marine 1922 (bio 'min. of marine, 1921-22'). The wealthy-barrister-holding-a-foreign-consulship pattern is consistent, not contradictory.

evidence_cited.fact: F. H. D. Bell | Minister of Internal Affairs
Minister of Immigration and Leader of the Legislative Council
Sir F. H. D. Bell | Minister of Marine
Sir Francis Henry Dillon Bell | Consul (for North Island)

would_overturn: Evidence that the 1897-1909 consul was a different (e.g. older or younger) Francis Henry Dillon Bell whose listing was inherited — for instance a consular appointment record naming another holder — would detach the early consul rows, though the ministerial rows and the post-1917 knighted consul rows would remain his.
notes: Both stints are fragments of the same person, Rt. Hon. Sir Francis Henry Dillon Bell (b. 1851, NZ prime minister May 1925), split by name-key variants ('Francis Bell' vs 'F. H. D. / Francis Henry Dillon Bell'); they should be merged downstream. The name-trap here is his father Sir Francis Dillon Bell, K.C.M.G., whom the first stint's cluster key superficially matches — excluded by chronology (stint years 1920-22) and by the office bundle matching the son's bio verbatim. The honours columns (K.C.M.G. from 1917, never G.C.M.G.) lag the bio's 1923 G.C.M.G. only because the stint ends in 1922.

arguments_against: Strongest case against: the dossier flags the single-initial namesake trap (early entries print only 'ARUNACHALAM, P.'), a second Arunachalam exists in the staff lists (plausibly a son or kinsman in the same service), and the 1894 stint's deterministic place check failed because its supporting bio events inherit 'Ceylon' from an earlier clause. This fails on the office-sequence evidence, which is specific and dated. For the 1878 stint: the staff list shows 'Commissioner of Requests, Police Magistrate' from 1878 and 'District Judge, Commissioner of Requests and Police Magistrate' in 1888, matching the biography's 'comsrr. of requests, pol. mag, 1876' and 'dist. judge, June, 1887' (1885 in an earlier version). For the 1894 stint: 'Commissioner of Requests' at Colombo 1894-1898 matches 'comsrr. of requests, Colombo, 1892'; 'Registrar-General' from the 1899 edition matches 'registr.-gen., Jan., 1898'; the anomalous 1905 row 'District Judge, Commissioner of Requests, Police Magistrate' matches his documented 1903-1905 interlude as 'officer of class I, dist. judge, Kurunegala, 23rd June, 1903' before 'registr.-gen., Feb., 1905'; and the stint terminates in 1913, the year the biography prints 'ret., 1913'. A namesake would have to replicate this entire dated sequence including the unique colony-wide post of Registrar-General. The biography's forename is fully printed (Ponnambalam) in later editions, and the age gate is consistent throughout. No live counter-hypothesis survives for either stint, and the two stints are the same continuous career with a listing gap 1889-1893.

evidence_cited.fact: District Judge, Commissioner of Requests and Police Magistrate
District Judge, Commissioner of Requests, Police Magistrate

would_overturn: Evidence that the second Arunachalam in the staff lists also served as Commissioner of Requests or Registrar-General with initial P. in these same years, or a record placing Ponnambalam Arunachalam outside Ceylon during 1878-1888 or 1894-1913.
notes: Sir Ponnambalam Arunachalam, b. 1853. Single-initial warning is mitigated by full forename in later editions, near-unique surname (2 officials, 1 biography), and a dated office sequence (police magistrate -> district judge -> commissioner of requests Colombo -> registrar-general -> district judge Kurunegala interlude -> registrar-general -> retired 1913) that both stints jointly reproduce. The stint end in 1913 coincides exactly with the printed retirement.

arguments_against: Strongest case against: this stint is visibly a conflation of more than one Boyd. The 'John Boyd' rows listed as 'Senator' (1883, 1889, 1890) and the 'J. Boyd' rows as 'Government Agent' (1877-79) carry no middle initial/name and describe offices the biography never mentions; the historical Senator John Boyd of New Brunswick was a different man, and a Chancellor of Ontario does not simultaneously sit as a Senator. If I assign the whole stint, I attribute those rows to the Chancellor — a partial false merge. Why the assignment still stands at stint level: the stint's key name is the full 'Boyd, John Alexander', and the dominant, continuous record (Chancellor, 1883-1915, 'John A. Boyd'/'John Alexander Boyd') matches the biography on every dimension — 'Chancellor of Ontario' vs the bio's 'chan. of Ontario, 1881', the K.C.M.G. carried in the 1913-15 rows matching the bio's honours, departments 'High Court of Justice' matching 'pres. of high ct. of just., 1887', and the 1913-15 rows coinciding with the bio's edition span. The identity of the stint's principal is not in doubt; the doubt is row-level contamination, which caps confidence at probable so a human can strip the Senator/Government Agent rows.
would_overturn: Evidence that the Chancellor rows belong to a different John A. Boyd than the K.C.M.G. biographee (effectively none conceivable), or — more realistically — a decision to split the stint, which would remove the Senator and Government Agent rows from this person without overturning the core identity.
notes: REVIEWER: this stint conflates at least two and possibly three people under the Boyd key. Rows printed 'John A. Boyd'/'John Alexander Boyd' as Chancellor (1883-1915) are securely the biographee (chan. of Ontario 1881, K.C.M.G.). Rows printed 'John Boyd' as Senator (1883, 1889, 1890; Senate of Canada) and 'J. Boyd' as Government Agent (1877-1879) very likely belong to other Boyds (38 share the surname in the staff lists) and should be split out. Confidence held at probable solely because of this row-level contamination; the principal identity match itself is beyond reasonable doubt.

arguments_against: The single-initial namesake trap is live: 'DONNAN, J.' could in principle be a different J. Donnan from the staff-list line, e.g. a relative in Ceylon harbour service or one of the 2 same-surname biographies in the corpus. This fails for three reasons. First, Master Attendant is not a generic junior title; it is a singular senior harbour post (the staff list shows exactly one Master Attendant line, in the Harbour Department), and the bio explicitly holds that exact post at Colombo from 1863. Second, the stint's run (1877-1900) sits entirely inside the bio's stated tenure (appointed 1863, 'ret., 1902.') and terminates just before the retirement date, exactly as it should. Third, the corpus contains only 1 official with this surname in the staff lists, so there is no second staff-list Donnan for the bio to belong to. Two different J. Donnans successively holding the unique Master Attendant post at Colombo within the bio's own attested tenure is not a realistic hypothesis.
would_overturn: Evidence of a second J. Donnan in Ceylon harbour service in 1877-1900 (e.g. a separate staff-list line), or a record showing the biographical J. Donnan left the Master Attendant post before 1877 with a successor of the same initial, or a birth/death record incompatible with service through 1900.
notes: Single-initial biography, so the namesake trap was weighed explicitly. The required strong independent dimension is a specific position match on a unique senior post: bio 'mast. attendant, Colombo, Ceylon, 1863 ... ret., 1902' vs staff-list 'Master Attendant', Harbour Department, listed 1877-1900. Tenure containment is exact (stint ends 1900, retirement 1902). Minor staff-list OCR variants ('Muster Attendant', 'Master Attendants') are obvious typesetting/OCR noise on the same line.

arguments_against: Strongest case against: the bio is flagged as a single-initial namesake trap, and 'Clerk' is a generic junior title, so the 1889-1907 stint could in principle be a different E. Bonavia (12 Bonavias in the staff lists); the bio also never prints the middle initial 'L'. Why this fails: the 1896 staff list prints the full forename 'Edgar L. Bonavia', and the career matches the printed biography clause-for-clause across two decades and two stints: post office clerk (bio 1886; staff list 1889 'Clerk, Post Office'), transfer to the secretariat 1894 (1896-1905 clerk in the Chief Secretary's Office), Lieutenant Governor's Office by 1906-07, then 'dep. asst. sec. to govt., 1911' (1912 'Deputy Assistant Secretary'), 'asst. sec. to govt., 1912' (1913-1921 'Assistant Secretary'), the shared C.M.G. honour (bio 1917; staff list from 1918), and 'perm. sec. to head of ministry, 5th Nov., 1921' (1922-1925 'Permanent Secretary, Office of the Head of the Ministry'). These are many independent agreeing dimensions (full forename, specific successive positions, exact departments, dates, honour); no rival Bonavia hypothesis survives. The two stints are continuous segments of the same documented career, with the 1908-1911 gap in stint coverage bridged by the bio's 'stenographer to the coun. of govt., 1891-1911'.

evidence_cited.fact: Office of the Head of the Ministry

would_overturn: Evidence of a second 'Edgar Bonavia' in Maltese government service in the same decades (e.g., a son with the same forename serving in the secretariat), or a printed source showing the C.M.G. of 1917 was awarded to a different E. Bonavia.
notes: The two Edgar L. Bonavia stints (1889-1907 and 1912-1925) are clearly the same man as the biography; the deterministic position_sim failures are string-similarity artifacts ('clk., post office' vs 'Second Class Clerk'; 'boy scouts' comsnr.' vs 'Assistant Secretary') -- the verbatim texts agree precisely once abbreviations are expanded. The 'Miss E. Bonavia' stint should be kept as a separate person record.

arguments_against: Strongest case against: Macpherson is very common here (29 officials, 11 biographies), the bio has no printed birth year, position_sim failed mechanically, and the stint contains rows that cannot be this man — 'Lieut.-Col. Macpherson | Accountant | Militia and Defence Department' (1877, 1878) is a serving departmental accountant, not a sitting senator; that is a second individual swept into the cluster by surname. One could extend the doubt to the whole stint. That extension fails for the senator rows: the bio's subject was 'speaker of the senate till 1883' and the staff list shows 'D. L. Macpherson | Speaker of the Senate (without Portfolio)' in the Queen's Privy Council list and 'Speaker' of the Senate in exactly 1883 and no other year — a unique office with exact date agreement; he was 'summoned by royal mandamus to a seat in the senate', matching the continuous Senator listings; and the staff lists acquire 'Sir' and 'K.C.M.G.' from 1886, immediately after the bio's K.C.M.G. (1884). A rival D. L. Macpherson who was simultaneously Senator, Speaker in 1883, Privy Councillor, and K.C.M.G. is not a live hypothesis. Confidence is held at probable solely because the stint cluster is contaminated by the Militia accountant rows and should be split before the link is published.

evidence_cited.fact: Sir D. L. Macpherson | Senator | The Queen's Privy Council for Canada | K.C.M.G.

would_overturn: Evidence of a second David L. Macpherson in the Canadian Senate in this period, or a record showing the Speakership of 1883 was held by someone other than the bio's subject; alternatively, confirmation that the Lieut.-Col. accountant rows are the same man would force a full re-examination.
notes: Identity of the senator rows is effectively beyond doubt (unique 1883 Senate Speakership, Privy Council membership, K.C.M.G. appearing in listings from 1886 after the 1884 award). The verdict is probable rather than certain because the stint cluster is contaminated: the 1877-78 'Lieut.-Col. Macpherson, Accountant, Militia and Defence Department' rows are a different person (no forename printed, incompatible occupation) and should be split out upstream. The bio's 'resigned 1885' refers to leaving the ministry, not the Senate, so the Senator listings through 1896 are consistent.

arguments_against: Strongest case against: (1) The bio's only specific office is 'sec. for customs, Tasmania, 1894', while the staff list for 1894 prints 'Collector and Landing Surveyor' — Secretary and Collector are not the same office, so the bio could describe a different Barnard heading the customs secretariat while this one collected at the port. (2) The stint chain itself looks conflated: in 1877-1879 it contains two concurrent rows, 'James Barnard, Government Printer' and 'J. Barnard, Warehouse-keeper, Customs Department' — one person cannot hold both rows, and the Government Printer is plausibly an older same-name relative, so part of this stint may not belong to anyone in this dossier. (3) The corpus has 32 Barnard officials and 13 Barnard bios, and the bio is effectively single-initial-grade ('James' is common). Why it still fails to block the link: the customs trajectory in the stint (Warehouse-keeper 1877 -> Senior Landing Waiter 1888 -> Collector and Landing Surveyor exactly in 1894 -> Collector and Inspector to 1909) agrees with a man who 'Ent. govt. service, 1864' and whose one career milestone is a senior customs post in Tasmania dated exactly 1894; the promotion-year agreement (1894), the colony (Tasmania), the department (Customs), the forename/initial, and 4 co-occurring editions (1905-1908) are multiple independent dimensions all pointing the same way. The Secretary/Collector title gap and the Government-Printer contamination keep this below 'certain'.
would_overturn: A staff list or bio showing a separate 'Secretary for Customs' Barnard in Tasmania around 1894 distinct from the Collector; or evidence that the bio's James Barnard served in a non-customs department in 1877-1909; or a birth/death date making the 1864 entry or the 1909 listing chronologically impossible for the customs man.
notes: The stint chain appears internally conflated: 1877-1879 contain two concurrent rows (James Barnard, Government Printer, Miscellaneous Offices vs J. Barnard, Warehouse-keeper, Customs Department). The Government Printer rows likely belong to a different (probably older, same-name) James Barnard; the customs rows are the ones matching this biography. Recommend splitting the chain before publication; the 'probable' confidence applies to the customs career, not the Government Printer rows.

arguments_against: Strongest counter-case: the bio carries bare initials ('J. L.'), Vanderstraaten is a Ceylon Burgher family name with 4 officials in the staff lists, and 'Colonial Surgeon' is a graded rank held by several officers at once, not a unique office — so either stint could belong to a same-initialled relative, and in particular the 1898-99 stint could be a son who reached Colonial Surgeon grade after the bio's man retired (his bio gives no retirement date, and entries persist after retirement). Against this: (1) the 1878-1894 stint's grade transition — 'Assistant Colonial Surgeon' through 1883, 'Colonial Surgeon' from 1886 — lands exactly on the bio's 'colonial surgeon, 1885' promotion, with the preceding assistant-grade years matching 'assistant colonial surgeon, second class, 1867; first class, 1868'; only one J. L. Vanderstraaten row exists per year, so if these rows were a relative, the bio's man (holding the identical grades in the identical years) would be inexplicably absent from his own department's list. (2) For 1898-99, the same printed name resumes in the same grade after a 4-year extraction gap, and the bio (still describing him as colonial surgeon and principal of the medical college) is reprinted in those same 1898 and 1899 editions; no junior-grade rows for any second 'J. L.' appear anywhere in the case to support a successor hypothesis. The relative-successor hypothesis for 1898-99 is not fully killable from this dossier alone, which is why the verdict stays at probable rather than certain.
would_overturn: Evidence that one of the other 3 Vanderstraatens in the staff lists also bore the initials 'J. L.' (e.g. a son in the medical department), a retirement or death date for the bio's man before 1898, or staff-list rows showing two simultaneous J. L. Vanderstraatens in any year.
notes: The two stints are almost certainly one continuous career split by an 1895-1897 extraction gap (same printed name, same grade on both sides). The 1878-1894 stint on its own approaches certain via the 1885 promotion-timing match; the 1898-99 tail is the residual risk that holds the package at probable. If human review confirms no second J. L. Vanderstraaten, upgrade to certain.

arguments_against: Strongest case this is wrong: the bio carries a single initial ('A.'), Byrne is common in this corpus (20 staff-list officials, 10 biographies), and the stint prints an extra initial 'X' (1879-1886) that the biography never shows — so the staff-list 'A. X. Byrne', a mere Clerk in the Civil Establishment, could be a different man (a brother or cousin) while the bio's A. Byrne spent 1880-1893 as an itinerant private secretary to successive governors and administrators. Also, 'Clerk' is exactly the generic junior title the instructions warn about, and the mechanical position_sim 100 ('Governor's clerk' ~ 'Clerk') is over-generous. Why the link nonetheless holds on balance: the stint is not a generic clerkship — it is repeatedly 'Clerk, Governor's Office' (1880, 1883, 1889, 1890, 1894), the exact substantive post the bio gives ('Governor's clerk, Natal, Mar., 1878'), opening in 1879 immediately after the bio's appointment; the private-secretary duties in the bio (to officers administering the government, to the governor in 1890, to the administrator in 1893) are precisely the special duties a Governor's Office clerk performs while the staff list keeps printing his substantive post; and the stint's 22-year span (1879-1900) is co-extensive with the bio's edition run (1886-1900), both ending together around 1900. The 'X' is consistent with a middle initial the biography simply omits. A second A. Byrne serving simultaneously as the Governor's Office clerk is not evidenced anywhere in the dossier. The counter-hypothesis is weakened but not impossible, hence probable rather than certain.

evidence_cited.fact: priv. sec. to gov. Natal, Feb., 1890

would_overturn: Any record giving the bio subject's full forename(s) inconsistent with 'A. X.' (e.g., a forename with no X middle name), or a staff list/biography showing a second contemporaneous A. Byrne attached to the Natal Governor's Office or Civil Establishment in 1879-1900.
notes: Sent to human review per 'probable' semantics. Decisive check for a reviewer: Natal civil lists or Blue Books giving the full name of the Governor's Office clerk (is he Alfred Xavier Byrne or similar?), and whether any of the other 9 Byrne biographies claims clerk/Chief Clerk service in the Natal Civil Establishment in the 1890s.

arguments_against: Williams is the weakest possible surname signal (380 officials, 162 biographies share it) and the bio gives initials only. The strongest counter-hypothesis: the stint chain conflates TWO D. J. Williamses in Bermuda's education service — the bio prints 'inspr., schs., Berm., 1946', yet the staff list shows D. J. Williams as 'Organiser of Physical Education' in 1949-1951 and as Inspector of Schools only from 1953; if the bio subject was already an inspector in 1946, the 1949-51 organiser rows could be a different man. Why I still assign: the chain is a single continuous name in one small colony's Education department with a natural promotion ladder (PE organiser -> Inspector of Schools -> Director of Education), no year shows two D. J. Williamses simultaneously, and the anchor matches are very strong and unique — 'dir., educ., 1956' against 'Director of Education' listed 1957-1966, and 'inspr., schs.' against 'Inspector of Schools' 1953-55 — with 10 co-occurring edition-years. The 1946-vs-1949 title discrepancy is most plausibly bio compression or retitling, but it is a real tension, so this stays below 'certain'.
would_overturn: Evidence of two contemporaneous D. J. Williamses in Bermuda's Education department (e.g. an edition listing both an Organiser of Physical Education and an Inspector of Schools named D. J. Williams), or a primary source showing the bio subject held the inspectorship continuously from 1946 while someone else was the PE organiser.
notes: The Director of Education and Inspector of Schools rows (1953-1966) match the biography decisively. The 1949-1951 'Organiser of Physical Education' rows are the residual uncertainty: the bio claims an inspectorship from 1946, which those rows do not show. If the chain is ever split, the 1953-1966 portion is the safe core.

arguments_against: Strongest case this is wrong: 'Pace' is an extremely common Maltese surname (20 officials with it in the staff lists), the biography carries a single initial only, and a different L. Pace could have held judicial registrarships. Why it fails: the bio names two highly specific offices with appointment years — 'registr., Gozo courts, 1932' and 'registr., courts of jud. police, Malta, 1941' — and the two assigned stints trace exactly that career. The 1934 stint is 'Registrar' in the Judicial Establishment, explicitly 'Registrar, Gozo' by 1939-40, and vanishes after 1940, precisely when the bio says he transferred to the courts of judicial police (1941). The 1949 stint then resumes as 'Registrar, Courts of Judicial Police' (position_sim 89) through 1957, co-occurring with the bio in editions 1955-1957. There is only one such registrarship at a time; for the counter-hypothesis to hold, a second L. Pace would have to hold the same singular post the bio claims for the same years, which the staff lists do not show. The single-initial trap is overcome by a specific, non-generic position match in both eras plus seamless career-shape continuity across the 1941 transition.

evidence_cited.fact: registr., courts of jud. police, Malta, 1941

would_overturn: A staff list or biography showing a second L. Pace holding the Gozo or Courts-of-Judicial-Police registrarship in 1934-1940 or 1949-1957, or evidence (e.g., a death notice or different birth year) that the Gozo registrar of the 1930s and the judicial-police registrar of the 1950s were different men.
notes: The 1941-1948 gap between the two assigned stints coincides with the bio's stated 1941 transfer; wartime edition coverage is sparse in that window. The 1915 customs stint may belong to an older relative or unrelated namesake.

arguments_against: Strongest case against: (1) the dossier flags a single-initial namesake trap and there are 53 Pearsons in the staff lists, so this could be another Pearson clerk in Jamaica; (2) 'Clerk' is a generic junior title; (3) from 1905 the stint table contains 'T. L. Pearson' rows (2nd/1st Class Superintendent, Public Works) which coexist with 'T. Pearson' (Chief Clerk, Medical Department) in 1905-1908, proving the stint record bundles at least two distinct men in its later years. Why the core link survives: the namesake warning is misapplied here -- the staff list prints the FULL forename 'Turner Pearson' (the bio's 'TORNER' is a trivial OCR error for 'Turner'), and the uncommon forename plus the exact, highly specific dual posting (clerk at BOTH the Public Hospital and the Lock Hospital simultaneously, 1877-1883, precisely matching the bio's 'Chief clerk and purveyor, public hospital, and clerk to the lock hospital ... April, 1875') and 3 agreeing edition co-occurrences are multiple independent dimensions. The conflation of T. L. Pearson into the same stint is why confidence is held at probable rather than certain.
would_overturn: Evidence that a second 'Turner Pearson' served in Jamaica's hospital service in the late 1870s, or that the 1877-1900 hospital-clerk rows belong to a different forename expanded elsewhere as something other than Turner. Also, confirmation that the 1905-1921 'T. L. Pearson' Public Works rows are intended as part of this stint's identity would force the stint to be split before assignment.
notes: The stint record appears internally conflated: 'T. Pearson' (Chief Clerk, Medical Department) and 'T. L. Pearson' (2nd Class Superintendents, Public Works) are listed simultaneously in 1905-1908, so they are two different people. The biography matches the hospital/Medical Department line (1877 through ~1908). The 1909-1921 Public Works 'T. L. Pearson' rows almost certainly belong to a different man and the stint should be split upstream. Assignment is made for the stint as the 'Turner Pearson' hospital clerk identity; the bio's printed forename 'TORNER' is an OCR error for 'Turner'.

arguments_against: Strongest case against: this is a single-initial biography ('WATT, J.') with 39 Watt officials and 20 Watt biographies in the corpus, so the staff-list 'J. Watt' could be a different man — for instance a younger relative or a successor chaplain — and the stint runs to 1900, twelve years after the biography stops being printed (1888), so the later rows could even belong to a successor folded into one stint. This largely fails because the dimensions agree on more than a generic title: the biography is a Presbyterian colonial chaplain ('Colonial chaplain of the Scot's Kirk, Kandy, Ceylon, 1866'), and the staff list shows J. Watt as 'Colonial Chaplain' in Ceylon's Ecclesiastical Department, listed in 1888 with the title 'Rev.' under the 'Presbyterian Church' heading — denomination, office, and colony all match, and the Presbyterian colonial chaplaincy establishment in Ceylon was very small. The biography appears in editions 1883, 1886, 1888, precisely the years the staff list carries this chaplain. The surviving residual risk (single initial; possible undetected succession by another Rev. J. Watt within the 1877-1900 stint) keeps this at probable rather than certain.
would_overturn: Evidence of a second Rev. J. Watt in the Ceylon Presbyterian chaplaincy in 1877-1900, or a record that the Scot's Kirk chaplain of 1866 died or left Ceylon before 1877.
notes: Single-initial namesake trap acknowledged. The merge rests on denomination + office + colony agreement (Presbyterian colonial chaplain, Ceylon) concurrent with the biography's edition span, not on tenure overlap alone. Routed to human review per 'probable' semantics.
